title: CRP Appeal — Bent County — Grassland Contracts
advisoryNotes: Heard March 24, 2026. April 23 follow-up: confirm written STC decision text (must address dual-528-plan ambiguity on Contract 11117 and missing 528 page for Contract 11101), confirm NAD right-to-appeal notice delivered to producers within 30 days of final determination, confirm Bent County CED has refund-calculation worksheet if affirmed.
issueSummary: Bent County COC terminated 7 CRP grassland contracts (11071, 11075, 11101, 11114, 11115, 11116, 11117) for noncompliance with grazing plans. All NRCS field visits occurred during winter dormancy (Jan–Mar 2025). Producers never provided grazing records despite multiple requests. COC found insufficient stubble height, missing species diversity, and no good faith effort. Full refund of all payments assessed. Continued from March 24 STC.
fullText:
Appellants John and Stephanie Ebright had Grassland CRP contracts accepted for sign-ups 204 and 206. All contracts, conservation plans (NRCS-CPA-1155), and 528 CRP Grasslands Prescribed Grazing Plans were signed timely.

Feb 10, 2025 — Baca County CED requested grazing records. No records received.
Apr 22, 2025 — Noncompliance letter. NRCS visits Jan 31, Feb 26, Mar 11, 2025 (all winter dormancy) showed bare ground and hard-grazed fields.
May 6, 2025 — Producers provided good faith letter.
May 20, 2025 — COC determined no good faith: stubble height below 4–6", species diversity insufficient, no grazing records.
Jun 17, 2025 — Termination letters issued.
Jul 10, 2025 — Producers requested reconsideration.
Aug 5, 2025 — Reconsideration hearing. Producers stated they did not receive Feb 10 letter. Showed July 1 photos. Noted 2 ft snow in December, sold 40 head.
COC upheld termination.
Mar 24, 2026 — First STC review. Continued to April 23, 2026.
theGood: All three NRCS field visits were during winter dormancy (Jan 31, Feb 26, Mar 11). Dormant-season stubble height is not an accurate measure of growing-season grazing compliance. The 4–6 inch standard in the 528 plan is an active-grazing indicator, not a post-dormancy measurement.
Producers provided July 1, 2025 photos showing representative cover on the contracts — reviewed and dismissed by COC without documented rebuttal in the record.
Producers stated they did not receive the February 10 letter requesting grazing records. If not received, failure to produce records cannot be characterized as willful noncompliance.

Sale tickets from La Junta Livestock Commission document 39 head of Ebright cattle sold between February 5 and March 12, 2025 (checks #100519, #101096, #101530; total weight 39,460 lbs). This is documentary corroboration of the 'sold 40 head' corrective-action claim in the COC record — a verifiable step-down in stocking following agency notification. Check #101096 shows FSA and Fellowship Credit Union listed as lienholders, further confirming the sale was formal and disclosed.
Two feet of December snow provides a plausible, verifiable explanation for suppressed ground cover appearance in January–March field visits.
Prior STC (Dec 2025) granted equitable relief in Moffat County CRP misaction case — direct Colorado STC precedent for relief exists.
Seven active CRP contracts represents substantial ongoing program engagement and economic incentive to comply.
Case was continued from March 24 STC — sufficient complexity already recognized by prior STC panel.
theBad: Grazing records were not provided contemporaneously — they were submitted on August 11, 2025 via email from Stephanie Ebright, 6 days AFTER the reconsideration hearing and nearly 4 months after the termination decision. The cover email explicitly states the records were 'pieced together from memory' because the originals 'got deleted' from a computer and could not be recovered. COC never had these records during the dispositive decisions. When produced, the records documented stocking at 400%–900% of planned capacity on multiple tracts — which undercuts any good-faith compliance claim.
All 528 plans were signed by the producers — explicit written acknowledgment of stocking limits and stubble height requirements.
Three independent NRCS field visits on separate dates all produced consistent findings of bare ground and hard-grazed fields.
Species diversity failure (30-point stand, 5 species, 3 native grasses) is a separate independent basis for noncompliance beyond grazing.
COC reviewed July 1 photos at reconsideration and upheld their finding — COC did not find the producers' explanation credible.
Claim of not receiving Feb 10 letter was not raised until the August 5 hearing, six months after the fact.
Grazing records request was sent by Baca County CED but records were still never produced even after the April noncompliance notice.

redFlags.text: All three NRCS field visits occurred during winter dormancy (Jan 31, Feb 26, Mar 11). Measuring stubble height during dormancy may not meet the evidentiary standard for a growing-season noncompliance determination. The 528 plan 4–6 inch standard is an active-grazing management indicator, not a post-dormancy measurement.

The grazing records request letter (Feb 10) was sent by Baca County CED, not Bent County CED. The record should clarify whether this was procedurally appropriate and whether certified delivery was used.

2-CRP (Rev.6) paragraph 603 requires COC to document the specific factors weighed in concluding no good faith effort — not just the absence of records. Affirmative in-season evidence of noncompliance should be present in the record.

Contract 11117/Tract 4328 has TWO 528 plans on file with no written FSA determination of which plan governed. Citing noncompliance against an unresolved standard is administratively defective for this contract.
If producers genuinely did not receive the February 10 letter, FSA cannot demonstrate adequate notice and opportunity to comply before the April 22 noncompliance determination was issued.

Producer-submitted grazing records (Stephanie Ebright, email 2025-08-11) document stocking substantially above 528 Prescribed Grazing Plan limits on multiple tracts for the 2024–2025 grazing period: Tract 772 / Contract 11075 — 40 head for ~4 months against a 10-head plan (~400%); Tract 2396 / Contract 11116 — 40–45 head for 4+ months against a 5-head plan (~800–900%); Tract 4328 / Contract 11117 — 46 head for ~7 weeks against a 41-head plan (~112%). The photographic evidence at winter visit dates showing zero or at-plan stocking captures only the post-reduction state (following the Feb–Mar 2025 sales), not the substantially overstocked condition documented by the producer's own records during the main grazing period.

commonSense: Seven contracts out of compliance simultaneously with zero grazing records produced over 14 months. The pattern is difficult to explain by accident — but winter dormancy measurements are not adequate physical evidence of growing-season violations.
The July 1 producer photos deserve more than a general dismissal. If they show adequate cover, that evidence should be specifically rebutted in the COC and STC record.

A producer managing 2,400+ CRP acres has substantial rental income at stake. Producing no grazing records over 14 months and multiple requests is either a profound oversight or intentional — that distinction is central to the good faith determination.
The two-feet-of-snow and 40-head-sale statements are independently verifiable. Neither was corroborated in the agency record prior to the determination.

contracts.note: 528 plan data not recovered from file split
TWO 528 plans on file — no written FSA determination of operative plan

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Full Relief
resolutionOptions.description: Reinstate all 7 contracts. Waive refund. Find dormant-season measurements insufficient for a compliance determination without in-season corroboration.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: HIGH — establishes precedent that CRP termination requires in-season evidence, not dormant-season spot checks.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW if denied — dormant-season measurement standard is a strong appellate argument.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Partial Relief
resolutionOptions.description: Reinstate contracts where agency evidence is weakest. Negotiate refund only on contracts with the clearest in-season documentation.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: MODERATE — requires contract-by-contract analysis at STC level.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: MODERATE.

resolutionOptions.label: Table — Require In-Season Monitoring
resolutionOptions.description: Suspend determination. Direct NRCS to conduct May–Sep 2026 monitoring visits to establish current condition baseline before final vote.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW — produces a better evidentiary record.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW.

resolutionOptions.label: Refer to Mediation
resolutionOptions.description: Refer to Colorado FSA mediation program before STC renders final determination.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: LOW — appeal rights preserved if mediation fails.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW.

resolutionOptions.label: Deny — Document Thoroughly
resolutionOptions.description: Affirm COC determination. Explicitly address dormant-season measurement issue and dual 528 plan issue for Contract 11117 in the STC written decision.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: HIGH NAD reversal risk if red flags not explicitly addressed in STC decision text.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: Must escalate to NAD to pursue further relief.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: HIGH — dormant-season measurement and dual-plan issues are strong appellate arguments.

issues.question: Are the CRP contracts out of compliance with the terms and conditions?
agencyPosition: Three NRCS field visits (Jan 31, Feb 26, Mar 11) documented bare ground and no stubble height remaining
CRP field worksheets indicated bare ground; status review sheets confirmed contracts were grazed hard
Photos taken by NRCS showed no remaining grass height
The 528 Grazing Plans required 4"–6" minimum stubble height as the indicator to remove livestock — this was not met
Species diversity requirements (30-point stand, 5 species, minimum 3 native grasses) were not represented in the field
Grazing records required by the 528 Prescribed Grazing Plan were never provided despite multiple requests
appellantPosition: Compliance review was conducted while grass was dormant (Jan–Mar) — did not reflect true cover condition during growing season
Producers moved cattle to other locations after being notified of noncompliance
Producers received 2 feet of snow in December which may have impacted grazing conditions
Sold 40 head of cattle to reduce stocking pressure
July 1, 2025 photos showed representative cover samples (taken during growing season)
Claim they never received the February 10, 2025 letter requesting grazing records
issues.regulation: 2-CRP (Rev.6) Par. 427 — Maintaining Approved Cover; Par. 603B — Examples of Noncompliance
issues.analysis: The 528 Grazing Plans specifically state: "stubble height may be used as an indicator to remove livestock in introduced pasture or seeded stands with minimal diversity, minimal stubble height to remove livestock is 4-6 inches." Three independent NRCS field visits across a 6-week period documented no compliance with this requirement. The dormant-grass argument may have merit for species identification but does not explain the complete absence of stubble height.

issues.question: Did the appellants show a good faith effort to comply with the terms and conditions?
agencyPosition: COC reviewed all documentation and determined NO good faith effort on two separate occasions (May 20 and Aug 5)
Grazing records — required to be kept each year for each pasture — were never provided despite being requested Feb 10 and again during the process
Without grazing records, COC could not determine if producers followed recommended stocking rates in the conservation plan
The extent of overgrazing (bare ground, no stubble) across ALL 7 contracts suggests systematic non-compliance, not an isolated incident
Species diversity shortfall indicates the offered 30-point stand was never established or maintained
appellantPosition: Submitted written good faith explanation on May 5, 2025
Took corrective action: sold 40 head, moved remaining cattle to other locations
Claim they never received the Feb 10 letter requesting records — thus absence of records should not be held against them
Provided photos from Jul 1, 2025 showing cover had recovered during growing season
Environmental factors: 2 feet of snow in December created unusual conditions
Attended reconsideration hearing with POA to present their case
issues.regulation: 2-CRP (Rev.6) Par. 603A, 603D, 603E, 603F — Noncompliance and Good Faith Determinations
issues.analysis: Good faith under Par. 603 requires COC to consider: (1) whether the participant submitted an explanation (they did — May 5 letter), (2) whether the noncompliance issue was corrected (partial — sold cattle and moved others, but grazing records were never produced). The COC considered all of this twice and twice determined no good faith. Key question for STC: Was the COC's determination reasonable given the evidence? The dormant-season timing of field visits is a legitimate concern, but 4"–6" stubble height should be measurable regardless of growth season.

issues.question: Was the termination of all 7 contracts the correct remedy?
agencyPosition: Under Par. 571A, COC must terminate ALL land under CRP-1 when participant is out of compliance on all land and COC determines no good faith effort
All 7 contracts were found out of compliance — not just some
Termination is mandatory ("must terminate"), not discretionary, when the conditions are met
appellantPosition: Termination of all 7 contracts is the most severe penalty

Under Par. 574A, termination requires refund of ALL annual rental payments plus interest, ALL cost-share payments plus interest, CRP-SIP plus interest, PIP plus interest, and potentially liquidated damages
The financial impact is substantial across 7 contracts and multiple tracts
issues.analysis: If all contracts were out of compliance and the COC's not-good-faith determination stands, Par. 571A makes termination mandatory — the COC had no discretion to impose a lesser remedy. The STC's decision therefore hinges on Issues 1 and 2. If STC finds good faith, Par. 603E allows a standard payment reduction instead of termination. If STC finds no good faith, the termination and full refund requirement under Par. 574A is mandatory.

issues.question: Were proper procedures followed by the county office?
agencyPosition: Feb 10 letter sent requesting grazing records
Three NRCS field visits conducted (Jan 31, Feb 26, Mar 11)
Apr 22 noncompliance letter gave 15-day response window per Par. 603D
Producer explanation reviewed at May 20 COC meeting
Termination notice sent Jun 17
Reconsideration hearing held Aug 5 per producer request
Final termination letter Sep 2
appellantPosition: Producers claim they never received the Feb 10 letter requesting grazing records
If not received, producers were denied the opportunity to provide records before the noncompliance determination
All field visits occurred during dormant season (Jan–Mar) — questions whether this provides an accurate assessment of cover condition
issues.regulation: 2-CRP (Rev.6) Par. 603A, 603D — Noncompliance notification and determination procedures
issues.analysis: The procedural sequence appears complete: notification, response opportunity, COC review, determination, reconsideration opportunity, and final determination. The Feb 10 letter claim is significant — if the letter was not received, the producers had no notice that records were being requested until the Apr 22 noncompliance letter. However, the 528 Grazing Plans themselves require that records be kept, so the obligation to maintain records existed independent of the Feb 10 letter. STC should examine whether the letter was sent certified/tracked.

keyMetrics.label: Approved max head capacity
keyMetrics.note: Aggregate of signed 528 Prescribed Grazing Plans across 6 of 7 contracts. Tract 751 (Contract 11101) pending — 528 data not recovered from file split. Tract 4328 (Contract 11117) has two 528 plans on file with no written FSA determination of which governs; Plan B (41 head / 120 days / all 1,047.81 ac) is operative in this total; Plan A would drop total to 49.

keyMetrics.label: Peak over-plan stocking (per producer records)
keyMetrics.note: Tract 2396 / Contract 11116 — producer's own records show 40–45 head for 4+ months against a 5-head plan. Tract 772 / Contract 11075 shows ~400% of plan. Tract 4328 / Contract 11117 shows ~112% of plan. Source: grazing records submitted Aug 11, 2025 (reconstructed from memory per producer cover email).
sources: Producer grazing records, Stephanie Ebright email 2025-08-11

keyMetrics.label: Head sold (documented)
keyMetrics.note: La Junta Livestock Commission sale tickets — check #100519 (1 head, 2/5), #101096 (10 head, 2/26), #101530 (28 head, 3/12). Total 39,460 lbs. Documentary corroboration of the 'sold 40 head' corrective-action claim.
sources: La Junta Livestock Commission sale tickets #100519, #101096, #101530

keyMetrics.label: Head remaining in operation
keyMetrics.note: Head still in the producers' overall operation after the sales. Against the ~99–100-head starting total (39–40 sold + 60 left), the producers shed ~40% of the herd in response to the noncompliance letter.

keyMetrics.label: Head currently on Ebright CRP ground
keyMetrics.note: Of the 60 remaining, 30 on the CRP ground at issue; the other ~30 moved to other locations. Current stocking of 30 against approved max of 87 is ~35% of capacity — post-reduction state only.

grazingRecords.sourceNote: Records reconstructed from memory per producer cover email; originals reportedly deleted from computer
Records reconstructed from memory per producer cover email
Records reconstructed from memory per producer cover email
Records reconstructed from memory; end date open
Records reconstructed from memory per producer cover email

grazingRecords.tractDescription: Horse Pasture North + North of House (combined)
grazingRecords.stockingSplit: combined tract grouping — no per-tract split in producer records

grazingRecords.tractDescription: Horse Pasture North + North of House (combined)
grazingRecords.sourceNote: Horses, not cattle. End date open.
Records reported cattle across two tracts jointly
Records reported cattle across two tracts jointly; end date open

cattleSales.note: Sold to John Ebright and Fellowship Credit Union & FSA — lienholder present on the sale
Includes 1 cripple head (BlkWF-SL-Cow, Tag 2469) and 1 steer (Tag 2472, 830 lbs)

cattleSalesAggregate.note: Documentary support for the 'sold 40 head as corrective action' claim in COC record. Sale tickets show 39 head across three dates; a fourth head may be on the unreconciled Tag 2201 line of check #101096 (to verify against original ticket).

title: LDP Appeal — Washington County — 2024 Pelt LDP (3R Lamb Feeding, Rule family)
appellants: 3R Lamb Feeding LLC, Connie Rule, Spence Rule, Sumner Rule
presenter: PENDING DATA — presenter not in case file Summary Outline
advisoryNotes: MATERIAL CONFLICT: Steve Raptopoulos must recuse. Per June 12, 2025 Washington County COC minutes (Agency Record p.10), the Colorado Lamb Processors plant where the pelts were delivered/slaughtered is co-owned by the Rule and Raptopoulos families. The conflict is not procedural — it is a fact in the case record that bears on the date of loss of beneficial interest.
issueSummary: Washington County COC disapproved 2024 LDP requests for unshorn lamb pelts filed by 3R Lamb Feeding LLC (46,309 head), Spence Rule (879 head), Connie Rule (7 head), and Sumner Rule (6 head). Wool LDPs from the same CCC-633 EZ filings were approved. The disapproval was based on loss of beneficial interest prior to the January 16, 2025 filing of CCC-633 EZ page 1 — pelts were delivered to Colorado Lamb Processors throughout 2024, before the agreement form was signed. Appellants argue they were not notified of the pre-loss-of-BI filing requirement. COC denied equitable relief citing a May 30, 2023 notice letter and the Terms and Conditions of the CCC-633 EZ itself.
fullText: On January 16, 2025, 3R Lamb Feeding LLC, Connie Rule, Spence Rule, and Sumner Rule submitted 2024 CCC-633 EZ Loan Deficiency Payment Agreement and Request forms for both ungraded wool and unshorn lamb pelts, together with Part M (Request for Wool, Mohair, or Unshorn Pelt LDP) and production evidence. The pelts had been delivered on multiple dates throughout 2024 to Colorado Lamb Processors in Brush, Colorado. On April 10, 2025, the Washington County COC determined beneficial interest in the pelts had been lost prior to the January 16, 2025 CCC-633 EZ page 1 filing, disapproved the pelt LDP requests, and approved the corresponding wool LDPs on the same applications (wool BI determined to be maintained at filing). Appellants requested reconsideration on May 5, 2025. At the June 12, 2025 reconsideration hearing, Abby Rule appeared on behalf of the producers and argued: (1) applicants were unaware of the pre-loss-of-BI filing requirement, (2) CED Page did not notify them until a March 18, 2025 email, (3) during the 2022 appeal the beneficial interest rule was never raised even though circumstances were similar, (4) the County Office gave inconsistent guidance on date of delivery vs. date of slaughter, and (5) there was no intent to deceive. The COC tabled and directed CED Page to research Equitable Relief and Misaction/Misinformation. On a July 2, 2025 between-meetings action memorialized from a June 17, 2025 email poll, the COC unanimously upheld the disapproval, finding that the May 30, 2023 letter and the CCC-633 EZ Part A Terms and Conditions provided adequate prior notice — precluding equitable relief. The June 12, 2025 minutes additionally state, of record, that Colorado Lamb Processors is co-owned by the Rule and Raptopoulos families and that this co-ownership affects the date beneficial interest is lost.
theGood: The CCC-633 EZ forms were filed January 16, 2025 — before the January 31 final availability date for unshorn pelts. Timing on the final availability deadline was met; the only timing failure alleged is against the pre-loss-of-BI rule.

Appellants showed consistent good faith: filed within the final availability window, provided production evidence, appeared at reconsideration, and articulated specific procedural confusion rather than contesting the rule itself.

The June 12, 2025 minutes (p.10) concede on the record that the County Office gave inconsistent guidance on date of delivery vs. date of slaughter for determining loss of beneficial interest, and that this 'will be addressed and corrected for 2025 going forward.' A contemporaneous admission of prior misinformation weighs in favor of Misaction/Misinformation relief under 7-CP (Rev. 4).

The same June 12 minutes acknowledge that because Colorado Lamb Processors is jointly owned by the Rule and Raptopoulos families, 'the date beneficial interest is lost for the lambs owned by the family, harvested at the plant' is affected — and the COC commits to 'ensure the correct date is used for 2025 forward,' implying the 2024 date determination may not have correctly accounted for the ownership structure.

The 2022 appeal involving the same family concerned AGI and production evidence timeliness — not beneficial interest. A producer reasonably reading the 2022 determination would not have been put on notice about the beneficial interest rule specifically. The COC's assertion that the 2023 letter provided notice is weakened by the fact that the 2022 case never put the BI rule in issue.

The COC approved the companion wool LDPs on the same applications. This confirms the producers were eligible in all other respects and their filings were procedurally complete — the only deficiency was pelt-specific timing.
theBad: CCC-633 EZ Part A Terms and Conditions, which the producer signs, states the pre-loss-of-beneficial-interest rule on the face of the form. Every producer who has ever signed a CCC-633 EZ has agreed to those terms.

The COC has documentary evidence — a May 30, 2023 letter sent during the 2022 appeal process — that addressed beneficial interest in writing to these exact producers. That letter predates the 2024 filing by well over a year.

Pelts were delivered to the slaughter plant on multiple dates throughout 2024. Under 2-LP Wool and Mohair par. 408A, direct sale to a slaughter lamb company for immediate slaughter loses beneficial interest on the earlier of delivery or actual slaughter. CCC-633 EZ page 1 was filed January 16, 2025 — after every pelt delivery in 2024. The timing failure is undisputed.

Handbook 8-LP (Rev. 2) has contained the beneficial interest rule since September 2010 — a 15-year-standing requirement. 'We weren't aware' is difficult to sustain against that record, particularly for a large commercial operation like 3R Lamb Feeding LLC (46,309 head).

The COC's equitable relief analysis under 7-CP (Rev. 4) was on point: Misaction/Misinformation relief requires that the producer relied on incorrect agency information. Here the producer did not ask and was not told anything incorrect about beneficial interest for 2024 — the claim is only that FSA did not volunteer the rule proactively. That is a harder standard.
CED Page's March 18, 2025 email was sent looking forward to 2025, not retroactive to 2024. It does not create a new claim of misinformation for the 2024 cycle.
The size disparity matters: 3R Lamb Feeding LLC requested payment on 46,309 head — a commercial-scale operation. STC approval would create a large payment obligation on a facial procedural failure.

redFlags.text: June 12, 2025 minutes (p.10) of record: Colorado Lamb Processors plant is co-owned by the Rule and Raptopoulos families. The plant is the site of delivery/slaughter at which beneficial interest is deemed lost. STC member Steve Raptopoulos must recuse from discussion and vote on Appeal 2 under the same 16-AO Par. 137B standard the Washington COC applies to itself.

The COC's final disapproval was memorialized via an email poll conducted on June 17, 2025, then formally adopted at a 'July 2, 2025 Executive Session in between meetings' with the Chairperson attending via TEAMS. Committee members may want to verify that the email-poll → between-meetings adoption pattern complies with 16-AO procedural requirements for adverse determinations; if not, a procedural remand is available independent of the merits.

The COC itself conceded on the record (June 12, 2025 minutes p.10) that County Office guidance on date of delivery vs. date of slaughter was inconsistent and required correction. That concession is direct evidence the Misaction/Misinformation bar has been met as to at least one fact material to the case (the date of loss of beneficial interest). The COC's later finding that Misaction/Misinformation 'would not apply' addresses only the beneficial-interest-timing-rule disclosure — not the date-of-loss determination that the COC separately admitted was affected by bad guidance.

A denial here on 'the rule was on the form' rationale sets a standard that extinguishes every Misaction/Misinformation claim on any LDP going forward, because CCC-633 EZ Terms and Conditions cover the full rule set. STC should be deliberate about whether that is the precedent it wants.

The May 30, 2023 letter relied upon by the COC as prior notice was sent during a 2022 appeal that was decided on AGI and production-evidence timeliness — NOT on beneficial interest. NAD may find that a notice buried in correspondence about a different issue does not satisfy the constructive-notice standard required to foreclose Misaction/Misinformation relief.

commonSense: Producers filed January 16, 2025 — fifteen days before the January 31 final availability deadline. They were trying to comply, they just filed against the wrong deadline. That is the shape of a procedural mistake, not an evasion.

The same applications produced approved wool LDPs and disapproved pelt LDPs. The producers didn't selectively misfile — they applied one rule to both commodities, and the rule for pelts is stricter because of how lamb slaughter delivery is treated under 2-LP par. 408A. A reasonable producer who didn't know about 2-LP par. 408A specifically could easily miss this.

The COC's own minutes concede bad County Office guidance on the date-of-loss question and commit to correcting it going forward. Denying relief while simultaneously admitting the agency contributed to the confusion is internally inconsistent.

The Raptopoulos plant co-ownership is material, not incidental. It means one of the STC members who would normally rule on this case has family ownership of the facility that is the trigger point for the entire legal question. That is the textbook reason recusal rules exist, and it needs to be acknowledged in the opening of the hearing, not assumed.

contracts.note: Commercial-scale operation. Largest exposure of the four appellants by three orders of magnitude.
Wool LDP from same CCC-633 EZ filing — approved because BI determined to be maintained at filing. Not on appeal; shown for context only.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Full Relief — All 4 Appellants, All Pelt LDPs
resolutionOptions.description: Reverse the Washington County COC determination. Approve the 2024 Pelt LDPs for all four appellants on the basis that (1) the COC conceded on the record that County Office guidance on the date of loss of beneficial interest was inconsistent, (2) the Raptopoulos-family co-ownership of the slaughter plant affects the date-of-loss determination and was not properly factored in, and (3) the 2023 letter relied upon for prior notice addressed a different issue (AGI/production evidence) and does not satisfy constructive notice on beneficial interest.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: HIGH — sets precedent that a COC admission of inconsistent guidance + unresolved plant-ownership facts triggers Misaction/Misinformation relief even where the controlling rule is on the face of the signed form. Payment exposure on 3R Lamb alone is substantial (46,309 head).
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW if denied on same facts — NAD is likely to find the procedural concessions and plant co-ownership material. Risk of NAD reversal on STC denial is above-average.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Partial Relief — Remand for Recalculation on Date of Loss
resolutionOptions.description: Remand to Washington County COC to redetermine the date of loss of beneficial interest taking into account the Rule/Raptopoulos co-ownership of Colorado Lamb Processors. If any pelt deliveries occurred after the January 16, 2025 CCC-633 EZ page 1 filing under a corrected date-of-loss rule (e.g., date of slaughter rather than date of delivery, where ownership overlap exists), those specific pelts would be eligible. All other pelts remain disapproved.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: MEDIUM — narrow remand addressing the specific factual inconsistency the COC itself flagged, without reversing the broader beneficial-interest doctrine. Preserves the CCC-633 EZ notice precedent.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: MEDIUM — relief may be partial or zero depending on recalculation. Procedurally clean.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW — responsive to the specific procedural defects identified in the record.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Partial Relief — Small Producers Only (Connie, Sumner, Spence)
resolutionOptions.description: Approve pelt LDPs for Connie Rule (7 head), Sumner Rule (6 head), and Spence Rule (879 head) on Misaction/Misinformation grounds. Deny 3R Lamb Feeding LLC (46,309 head) on the basis that a commercial-scale operation is held to a higher constructive-notice standard.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW-MEDIUM — small total payment exposure; distinguishes sophistication of appellant without reversing doctrine.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: HIGH for 3R Lamb Feeding LLC (denial upheld on largest requested amount).
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: MEDIUM — sophistication-based distinctions are defensible but require a clear articulation in the written determination. 3R Lamb may appeal to NAD individually.

resolutionOptions.label: Remand for Procedural Defect — Email Poll/Between-Meetings Adoption
resolutionOptions.description: Remand to Washington County COC to re-adopt the disapproval determination in a properly noticed executive session, without ruling on the merits. Pure procedural disposition; STC takes no position on beneficial interest or equitable relief at this stage.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW — no merits precedent created; procedural hygiene reinforced.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: LOW in terms of STC action, but HIGH in practical terms — the COC is likely to re-adopt the same determination on proper procedure, and appellants' only gain is timing and a reset appeal clock.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW — STC is taking no merits position.

resolutionOptions.label: Uphold Denial — All 4 Appellants
resolutionOptions.description: Affirm the Washington County COC determination in full. Find that (1) the beneficial interest rule appears on the signed CCC-633 EZ Part A Terms and Conditions, (2) the May 30, 2023 letter provided written notice to these specific producers, (3) the handbook rule has existed since 2010, and (4) Misaction/Misinformation does not apply where the producer did not rely on any specific incorrect agency statement for the 2024 cycle.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW — consistent with handbook language and CCC-633 EZ Terms and Conditions. Reinforces the signed-form-is-notice doctrine.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: HIGH — full denial; 3R Lamb loses 46,309 head in pelt LDP.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: ABOVE-AVERAGE — NAD may find (a) the 2023 letter insufficient because the 2022 appeal was about a different issue, (b) the COC's own concession of inconsistent date-of-loss guidance meets the Misaction bar on at least one fact material to the case, and (c) the unresolved Raptopoulos plant co-ownership is a procedural defect that should have been cured before final determination. NAD reversal probability is material.

issues.question: Did the Washington County COC err in disapproving the 2024 Pelt LDPs on beneficial-interest grounds?
agencyPosition: Under 2-LP par. 408A, direct sale to a slaughter lamb company for immediate slaughter loses beneficial interest on the earlier of delivery or actual slaughter
CCC-633 EZ page 1 was filed January 16, 2025; pelts were delivered throughout 2024 — every delivery preceded the filing
The CCC-633 EZ Part A Terms and Conditions, signed by each producer, states the pre-loss-of-BI rule on the face of the form
appellantPosition: Filing was made on Jan 16, 2025 — fifteen days before the Jan 31 final availability deadline; appellants were trying to comply with the deadline they were aware of
The same applications produced approved wool LDPs and disapproved pelt LDPs — the producers applied one rule to both commodities
A reasonable producer who didn't know about 2-LP par. 408A specifically could easily miss the pelt-specific timing rule
issues.regulation: 8-LP (Rev. 3) Par. 227 (Beneficial Interest); Par. 1001 (CCC-633 EZ Requests); 7 CFR 1421.6; 2-LP Wool & Mohair Par. 408A
issues.analysis: Pelts were delivered throughout 2024; CCC-633 EZ page 1 was filed January 16, 2025. If 'date of delivery' controls, BI was lost before filing for every pelt and disapproval is correct on its face. The procedural failure is undisputed; the question is whether Misaction/Misinformation, equitable relief, or the Raptopoulos plant co-ownership change the outcome.

issues.question: Did the County Office provide inconsistent guidance on date-of-delivery vs. date-of-slaughter as the triggering event for loss of beneficial interest?
agencyPosition: COC concedes on the record (June 12, 2025 minutes p.10) that guidance was inconsistent and that this 'will be addressed and corrected for 2025 going forward'
However, the COC's later finding states Misaction/Misinformation does not apply to the pre-loss-of-BI filing requirement — the producer was not given incorrect information about that specific rule
appellantPosition: The COC's contemporaneous admission of prior inconsistent guidance is direct evidence the Misaction/Misinformation bar has been met as to at least one fact material to the case
Inconsistent guidance on the date-of-loss question goes to the heart of when beneficial interest was lost — not a tangential issue
issues.regulation: 7-CP (Rev. 4) — Finality Rule and Equitable Relief; Misaction/Misinformation provisions
issues.analysis: COC admission (June 12, 2025 minutes, p. 10): 'The confusion between submission of Date of Delivery or Date of Slaughter when considering when beneficial interest is lost will be addressed and corrected for 2025 going forward.' This is a contemporaneous admission of prior inconsistent guidance — directly probative on Misaction/Misinformation relief, even if the COC's own analysis split the question.

issues.question: Does the Rule/Raptopoulos joint ownership of Colorado Lamb Processors affect the date-of-loss determination for 2024?
agencyPosition: COC acknowledges (June 12, 2025 minutes p.10) that the plant co-ownership 'does affect the date beneficial interest is lost for the lambs owned by the family, harvested at the plant'
COC commits to 'ensure the correct date is used for 2025 forward' — implying the 2024 date determination did not correctly account for the ownership structure
appellantPosition: If beneficial interest under joint ownership is determined by a different rule than direct-sale-to-slaughter, the 2024 date-of-loss may be later than the COC found — potentially after the Jan 16, 2025 filing for at least some pelts
issues.regulation: 2-LP Wool & Mohair Par. 408A; 8-LP (Rev. 3) Par. 227B (Definition of Conditions for Beneficial Interest — control + title)
issues.analysis: The COC's own minutes acknowledge the co-ownership is material but did not factor it into the 2024 determination. STC should consider whether a remand for recalculation under a corrected date-of-loss rule (per the COC's own framing) is warranted. Independently, STC member Steve Raptopoulos must recuse — the conflict is in the case record, not procedural hygiene.

issues.question: Was the May 30, 2023 letter sufficient prior notice to foreclose Misaction/Misinformation relief?
agencyPosition: COC sent a written letter on May 30, 2023 to these specific producers that addressed beneficial interest
The letter predates the 2024 filing by well over a year — producers had constructive notice of the rule
appellantPosition: The 2022 appeal on which the 2023 letter was sent was decided on AGI and production evidence timeliness — not beneficial interest
The CCC-633 EZ for the 2022 case was filed June 2, 2021, well before the 2022 harvest; the beneficial interest rule was never put in issue in 2022
A notice buried in correspondence about a different adverse determination may not satisfy the constructive-notice standard
issues.analysis: Whether a beneficial interest paragraph embedded in a letter about a different adverse determination satisfies constructive notice is a live question for STC. NAD may find that a notice tied to a different issue does not satisfy the constructive-notice standard required to foreclose Misaction/Misinformation relief — this is the most likely path to NAD reversal if STC denies.

title: NAP Appeal — Otero County — 2024 Mixed Forage IGS
issueSummary: Proctor filed a late notice of loss (21 days after normal harvest date) on 52.16 acres of irrigated mixed forage in Otero County, claiming heat and wind caused the 2024 production loss. A winter storm before the November 22 inspection made it impossible to verify the claimed disaster — the loss adjustor's FSA-501 Statement of Facts was left blank on the disaster cause question, an agency documentation failure. COC disapproved the NOL and AFP. A POA signature deficiency caused a 42-day procedural delay in 2025. Case pending since June 2025 awaiting STC appointment.
fullText:
Proctor holds 65/100 buy-up NAP coverage on Mixfg IGS. Original filing Oct 27, 2023 at 50/55; upgraded to 65/100 on Jan 19, 2024, before the Dec 1, 2023 application closing date — timely.

Nov 5, 2024 — NOL filed claiming Heat/Wind Jul 1–Oct 30, apparent Oct 31. Filed 21 days after Oct 15 normal harvest date. Late-filed. Mandatory field inspection triggered.
Nov 22, 2024 — LA inspection. Fields harvested. LA counted 17 bales. FSA-501 Statement of Facts did NOT address any disaster event — left blank on this critical question. Photos showed snow-covered fields. No evidence of heat or wind damage visible.
Dec 11, 2024 — COC disapproved NOL: (1) inadequate proof of disaster; (2) Otero County did not exceed D1 drought; (3) LA did not indicate disaster caused loss; (4) not similar to other producers.
Dec 10, 2024 — AFP filed.
Jan 13, 2025 — COC disapproved AFP — no acceptable NOL on file.
Jan 29, 2025 — POA Kris Demoss emailed reconsideration request.
Mar 12, 2025 — Reconsideration hearing. COC upheld disapproval.
Apr 15, 2025 — Proctor filed STC appeal request.
Jun 4–5, 2025 — STO rejected documents — signatures did not match FSA-211 POA on file.
Jun 16, 2025 — Corrected signatures received. Appeal deemed timely filed.
Apr 8, 2026 — STC hearing scheduled April 23, 2026 at 1:00 PM.
theGood: NOL filed only 21 days late — a narrow window that arguably still permitted meaningful inspection if a winter storm had not intervened.

The Loss Adjustor's FSA-501 Statement of Facts was LEFT BLANK on the disaster cause question. 1-NAP paragraph 575C requires the LA to document findings on late-filed NOLs. A blank FSA-501 is an agency documentation failure, not just a producer failure.
Producer has adequate and documented irrigation capacity (7.75 Highline Canal shares for 55–60 acres) — the inadequate irrigation resources ineligible cause of loss does not apply.
Consistent NAP participant with 5-year APH and documented production history — not an opportunistic first-time filer.
D1 drought was documented in Otero County during the claimed period.
Production loss was real and substantial: 11.99 tons actual versus historical average of 44–58 tons on comparable acres — approximately 75–80% yield reduction.
Unit 1528 may have been improperly granted 65/100 buy-up coverage by FSA without documented growing history — an agency processing error favoring the producer under equitable relief principles.
POA signature deficiency was a correctable technical defect. Producer re-filed within the required window. No substantive right was forfeited.
theBad: NOL filed 21 days late. Winter storm before the November 22 inspection made it physically impossible to verify heat or wind damage. 1-NAP paragraph 575C explicitly provides for denial when late-filed NOLs cannot be inspected to verify the claimed disaster condition.
The 1-NAP manual example is directly on point: snow fell before field inspection — COC cannot approve because the disaster condition cannot be verified by a field visit.
COC found the claimed event not similar to other producers in the area — a significant factual finding difficult to overcome without independent weather corroboration.
Producer initially reported 24 bales then corrected to 17, explaining the discrepancy as accidentally including alfalfa bales. Correction was accepted but affects production record credibility.
No corroborating producer claims in the area for the same heat/wind event during the claimed period.

redFlags.text: The LA's FSA-501 Statement of Facts was blank on the disaster cause question. 1-NAP paragraph 575C requires specific documentation of damage findings on late-filed NOLs. A payment denial based on lack of inspection verification is undermined when the agency's own inspector failed to complete the required documentation.

Unit 1528 growing history: the case file states no documentation exists to determine if the producer met the growing history requirement for 65/100 buy-up (paragraph 50.5, 7 CFR 1437.5(e)). If FSA accepted buy-up coverage on Unit 1528 without documented history, that is an FSA processing error. Under equitable relief principles, the producer should not be denied benefits due to the agency's own failure to verify eligibility at application.

Initial appeal documents were rejected for improper POA signature — a 42-day administrative delay attributable to a technical requirement, not producer bad faith. The appeal was ultimately accepted as timely filed after correction.

commonSense: A producer with 5+ years of documented irrigated mixed forage production, adequate water rights, and consistent NAP participation takes 1 cutting (17 bales) in a year with historical production of 55+ bales. That is a genuine production loss regardless of whether every procedural box was checked.

The FSA-501 blank is the most consequential fact in this case. The agency's field inspection was the gatekeeper for the late-filed NOL — and the agency's own inspector failed to document their findings on the key question. Placing the full burden of that failure on the producer is difficult to justify.

The Unit 1528 growing history issue should be investigated before a final denial is issued. If FSA accepted buy-up service fees for coverage that should not have been granted, that is a FSA misaction — not a producer error.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Full Relief
resolutionOptions.description: Find the LA's blank FSA-501 constitutes an agency documentation failure that undermines the basis for NOL disapproval. Find D1 drought conditions and the producer's production history support the claim. Approve both the NOL and AFP.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: HIGH — establishes precedent for approving late-filed NOLs where inspection timing was beyond producer control.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: MODERATE if denied — blank FSA-501 is a strong appellate argument.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Partial Relief
resolutionOptions.description: Approve the NOL on D1 drought basis. Direct COC to recalculate AFP at confirmed production (17 bales). Address Unit 1528 coverage history issue through programmatic relief if buy-up was improperly granted.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: MODERATE.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW to MODERATE.

resolutionOptions.label: Remand — Investigate Unit 1528
resolutionOptions.description: Remand to Otero COC to determine whether 65/100 buy-up on Unit 1528 was properly granted. Direct COC to reconsider the NOL with specific attention to the blank FSA-501 documentation gap.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW — produces a better record for either outcome.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW.

resolutionOptions.label: Deny — Late Filed NOL
resolutionOptions.description: Affirm COC. Late-filed NOL could not be properly inspected due to snow. 1-NAP paragraph 575C explicitly contemplates denial in this circumstance.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW procedurally. MODERATE if blank FSA-501 and Unit 1528 coverage issues are raised at NAD.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: Loss of 2024 NAP payment on 52 acres.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: MODERATE — blank FSA-501 and Unit 1528 improper coverage grant are viable NAD arguments.

title: NAP Appeal — La Plata County — 2025 Non-Irrigated Safflower
appellants: River Edge Dairy and Farm, LLC (DeRon Gillen)
issueSummary: River Edge Dairy planted non-irrigated safflower for seed on 185.48 acres near Hesperus, La Plata County in May 2025. Seeds sprouted then died — claimed cause is drought combined with high winds drying seed furrows faster than roots could develop. COC disapproved: (1) 2025 had more precipitation than 2023 and 2024, years when River Edge successfully grew safflower; (2) seeding rate of ~2.5 lbs/acre was dramatically below the recommended 15–30 lbs/acre; (3) neighbor Blue Horizons Farm had a different, later loss timeline. Unit 1528 has no documented growing history for buy-up coverage. Appellant submitted late documents to Box before the April 23 meeting.
fullText:
River Edge Dairy has successfully grown safflower in 2023 and 2024. Planted non-irrigated safflower May 3–5, 2025 near Hesperus in SW La Plata County.

Jun 4, 2025 — NOL filed claiming drought beginning May 28, apparent May 28.
Jun 11, 2025 — LA Anita Lazor inspected. Photos show only seeds and weeds — no safflower plants visible.
Jul 31, 2025 — COC discussed NOL. Requested soil moisture tests or farming practices statement.
Aug 27, 2025 — Producer provided documentation on farming practices. No formal soil moisture tests submitted.
Sep 22, 2025 — COC disapproved NOL and AFP as action between meetings per 1-NAP paragraph 51E.
Dec 4, 2025 — Full reconsideration hearing. DeRon and Jordan Gillen attended in person.

Hearing detail: DeRon passed around safflower seeds and provided agronomic context. Planting method: double disc opener, 30-inch rows, seeds planted into furrow groove. High May winds dried out furrows faster than roots could grow. Seeds sprouted initially then died. Soil moisture assessed by shovel (dig 12–18 inches, make a dirt ball) plus probe in March showing 21 inches of moisture. Seeds were 1 year old from 2024 crop — viability tested by germination in wet rag.

Rainfall at Kline station Apr 1–Jun 30: 2025 = 3.48 in, 2024 = 1.21 in, 2023 = 1.12 in. COC noted 2025 had more precipitation than years with successful crops.

Seeding rate: Producer stated 30,000 seeds/acre in 30-inch rows, approximately 2.5 lbs/acre. COC noted recommended rate is 15–30 lbs/acre. Blue Horizons Farm uses approximately 15 lbs/acre.

Neighbor comparison: Blue Horizons Farm planted Apr 18–May 1, apparent loss not until Jun 23, appraisal showed partially grown plants. River Edge apparent loss May 28, appraisal showed only seeds and weeds.

Unit 1528: approximately 150 acres on F2083 enrolled in EQIP year 2 of 3-year contract requiring a broadleaf rotating crop. Safflower was on the EQIP-approved list.

COC deliberated: Could not conclude drought was sole cause of loss per 1-NAP paragraph 51E. Upheld disapproval.
theGood: D2 drought designation existed in the SW La Plata County field area as of May 6, 2025. D2 is Severe Drought on the US Drought Monitor scale — a significant documented drought condition.
Seeds sprouted and then died — confirmed at the hearing. Post-germination desiccation from wind-driven furrow moisture loss is a legitimate mechanism of drought-related crop failure.
River Edge has documented safflower success in 2023 and 2024 on the same land — not a speculative crop choice or an area unsuited for production.
Unit 1528 is enrolled in an active EQIP contract requiring a broadleaf rotating crop. Safflower was EQIP-approved. Producer had a USDA conservation obligation to plant this field.

All other drought NOLs in La Plata County in 2025 were approved — safflower on River Edge was the ONLY disapproval. Producer was treated differently from every other 2025 drought claimant in the county.
Neighboring producer Blue Horizons Farm experienced safflower damage with an approved NOL — confirming drought and wind conditions were legitimately damaging to safflower in the area.
Denying NAP coverage for a crop planted to fulfill a separate USDA EQIP conservation obligation creates an inequitable cross-program outcome.
Producer demonstrated detailed safflower agronomy at the hearing — 40 years of farming experience, multi-year crop history, documented seed viability testing.
theBad: 2025 April–June precipitation (3.48 inches) was HIGHER than 2023 (1.12 in) and 2024 (1.21 in) — years when River Edge successfully grew safflower. This is COC's strongest factual argument.

Seeding rate of approximately 2.5 lbs/acre versus the recommended 15–30 lbs/acre. River Edge planted at roughly 10–15% of recommended density. Failure to follow good farming practices is an ineligible cause of loss under 1-NAP paragraph 51F.

River Edge's loss was apparent May 28 — only 23–25 days after planting. Blue Horizons Farm planted earlier in April but had no apparent loss until June 23, a full month later despite being neighbors within a few miles.
LA appraisal photos on June 11 showed only seeds and weeds — no growing plants at all. Blue Horizons appraisal showed partially grown plants plus dead seeds.
Unit 1528 has no documented growing history for 65/100 buy-up coverage. Coverage eligibility on this unit is unverified.
COC requested formal soil moisture tests. Producer provided only a description of their hand-shovel method — not documented soil moisture data.

redFlags.text: Unit 1528 growing history: the case file states no documentation exists to determine if the producer met the growing history requirement for 65/100 buy-up (paragraph 50.5, 7 CFR 1437.5(e)). If FSA accepted buy-up coverage on Unit 1528 without documented history, that is an FSA processing error. Under equitable relief principles, the producer should not be denied benefits due to the agency's failure to verify eligibility at application.

EQIP cross-program conflict: Unit 1528 is in an active EQIP contract that approved safflower as the required broadleaf rotation crop. Denying NAP coverage for a crop planted in compliance with a separate USDA conservation contract obligation creates an adverse inter-program outcome that should be reviewed under USDA equity principles.

COC cited 1-NAP paragraph 51E drought timing analysis but the record does not include a formal agronomic analysis of safflower's specific moisture requirements during the planting-to-rosette stage at dryland elevations near Hesperus. General precipitation comparisons from a nearby weather station are not a crop-specific drought analysis.

COC disapproved the NOL as action between meetings on September 22 — over 3 months after the LA inspection on June 11. The extended timeline between inspection and COC action should be documented in COC minutes explaining the delay.

commonSense: Total seasonal precipitation comparison is not a complete drought analysis for dryland strip-till safflower planted in late May. The critical question is whether 0.66 inches between April 28 and May 28, combined with documented high winds, was sufficient for germinated seeds in an exposed 30-inch furrow near Hesperus to develop root depth before desiccating. That is a different question from whether the season had adequate total rainfall.

The seeding rate issue is legally the cleanest basis for COC's denial. At 2.5 lbs/acre versus 15–30 recommended, the stand density question is real. However, the producer provided a detailed agronomic explanation for why they use lower density in their specific conditions — this deserved expert agronomic evaluation, not just a citation to general recommendations.

The EQIP obligation on Unit 1528 is the most sympathetic fact. The federal government required this producer to plant a broadleaf crop on those specific acres as part of an active conservation contract, the producer planted safflower from the EQIP-approved list, and now the government is denying insurance coverage for that crop's failure.

All other drought claims in La Plata County in 2025 were approved. River Edge was the only one denied. When a producer is treated differently from every other claimant in the same county in the same year, the basis for that distinction needs to be clearly documented in the record.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Full Relief
resolutionOptions.description: Find D2 drought plus documented high winds constitutes an eligible cause of loss. Find that post-germination seed death satisfies the directly cause destruction standard. Find that seeding rate is not determinative when seeds did in fact germinate.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: HIGH — establishes precedent that non-standard seeding rates do not preclude drought loss approval.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: MODERATE if denied.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Partial Relief — Unit 328 Only
resolutionOptions.description: Approve Unit 328 (38.01 ac with documented growing history). Deny or remand Unit 1528 (147.47 ac with no documented growing history) for growing history documentation review.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: MODERATE.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: Partial recovery — the larger unit is denied.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW to MODERATE.

resolutionOptions.label: Remand — Growing History and Agronomy Review
resolutionOptions.description: Remand to La Plata COC: (1) determine whether 65/100 buy-up on Unit 1528 was properly granted; (2) obtain agronomic expert input on low-density safflower seeding in this specific growing region; (3) revisit drought analysis using field-specific precipitation data.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW — produces a better record.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW.

resolutionOptions.label: Deny — Precipitation and Seeding Rate
resolutionOptions.description: Affirm COC. 2025 precipitation exceeded successful prior years. Seeding rate at approximately 2.5 lbs/acre versus 15–30 recommended constitutes failure to follow good farming practices — an ineligible cause of loss under 1-NAP paragraph 51F.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: LOW procedurally. MODERATE if EQIP conflict and Unit 1528 improper coverage grant are raised at NAD.
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: Loss of 2025 NAP payment on 185 acres.
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: MODERATE — EQIP obligation and Unit 1528 improper coverage grant are viable NAD arguments.

title: CRP Payment Reduction Waiver — El Paso County
presenter: Hunter A. Cleveland, Farm Program Specialist
advisoryNotes: Carry-over from March 24, 2026 Executive Session, page 659. Confirm the final vote is recorded at the April 23 meeting and that the written decision cites the specific Par. 603 factors considered.
issueSummary: Payne Legacy LLC (El Paso County) requested a waiver of the CRP Standard Payment Reduction assessed by the COC after a documented noncompliance event. The STC was asked to waive the reduction or affirm the COC assessment. Carried forward to April 23 for final determination and recording of the vote.
fullText: El Paso County COC assessed a Standard Payment Reduction against Payne Legacy LLC under 2-CRP Par. 603E following a noncompliance event on the producer's Grassland CRP tract. Payne Legacy requested relief via a waiver, asserting that the circumstances were outside the producer's reasonable control and that corrective action was promptly taken. The STC reviewed the request at its March 24, 2026 Executive Session (15 min allocated, page 659). Final vote was not recorded in the March 24 minutes and the item was carried to April 23, 2026 for determination. If granted, no refund of prior payments is required; if denied, the COC's payment reduction stands and Payne Legacy's NAD appeal rights are preserved.
theGood: Producer requested waiver through proper channels — good-faith procedural posture
Staff (Hunter Cleveland) prepared the case for STC consideration with full documentation
Penalty at issue is a standard payment reduction, not contract termination — the lesser remedy under Par. 603E
Waiver, if granted, preserves the underlying CRP contract and avoids triggering refund of prior payments
theBad: Granting a waiver sets precedent — STC must ensure consistency with prior waiver decisions
COC's underlying noncompliance finding was not challenged — only the reduction amount
Program integrity concern: serial waivers undermine COC enforcement authority

redFlags.text: Confirm whether Payne Legacy LLC was afforded the full 30-day notice and response window under 2-CRP Par. 603D before the reduction was assessed.

Apply the same good-faith analytical framework used in the Ebright case (2-CRP Par. 603A, 603D, 603E, 603F) — inconsistent treatment across the same meeting creates NAD-reversal exposure for the more severe decision.
Waiver decisions must be documented in writing with the basis cited — STC minutes must recite the factual findings that support or deny the waiver.

commonSense: A standard payment reduction is the mid-range remedy between full compliance and contract termination. If the producer corrected the issue promptly and the violation was narrow in scope, a waiver may serve program objectives better than a punitive reduction.
Ensure this case is decided on the same factual record standard as Ebright — both are CRP noncompliance matters reviewed the same day.
If the waiver is granted, document in the minutes the specific mitigating facts that justified it, so the decision does not read as arbitrary.

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Waiver
resolutionOptions.description: Waive the standard payment reduction. Document mitigating facts in minutes. Contract continues on normal payment schedule.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: Moderate — precedent for future waiver requests
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: N/A — producer has no NAD grievance if granted

resolutionOptions.label: Grant Partial Waiver
resolutionOptions.description: Reduce the payment reduction by a specified percentage in recognition of mitigating circumstances but preserve some program consequence.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: Low — proportional remedy
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW

resolutionOptions.label: Deny Waiver — Affirm COC
resolutionOptions.description: Affirm the COC-assessed standard payment reduction. Producer retains NAD appeal rights within 30 days.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: Low if COC record is complete and the Par. 603 factors are cited in the decision text
resolutionOptions.appellantRisk: Reduction applied to next annual payment
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: LOW–MEDIUM — depends on quality of COC record

title: CRP Misaction — Moffat County (Precedent Reference)

issueSummary: December 2025 STC granted equitable relief in a Moffat County CRP misaction case. Retained in this registry as a direct controlling precedent for the Ebright and Payne Legacy matters — both CRP noncompliance cases where the producer asserts agency error or incomplete agency record.
fullText: Prior STC action in December 2025: equitable relief granted in a Moffat County CRP misaction case. This is the nearest direct Colorado precedent for relief in a CRP compliance dispute and should be cited in the written determinations for any current CRP appeal where the STC grants relief. [PENDING DATA] — full case name, contract numbers, and written decision text not yet attached to this record.
theGood: Direct Colorado precedent for CRP equitable relief within the last 6 months
Establishes that this STC will grant relief when the agency record is incomplete or contains material error
theBad: [PENDING DATA] — cannot confirm the scope or facts of the Moffat case without the written decision

redFlags.text: Attach the December 2025 written determination to this record before citing it as precedent in any current appeal.

commonSense: Precedent carries weight only when the facts are known. Retrieve the December 2025 decision from the minutes and link it here before using it in deliberation.

Before the April 23 meeting, confirm with the State Office whether any new CRP, NAP, LFP, ELAP, or Common Provisions appeals have been received and require STC hearing.

Confirm timing: 30-day filing window from written COC decision (7 CFR 780.9); STC hearing scheduled at next regular meeting where time permits.
theGood: [PENDING DATA]
theBad: [PENDING DATA]

title: Otero/Crowley COC Administrative Action (NOT an appeal — context only)
voteRecorded: March 17, 2026 — Motion to cease administrative action PASSED. Motion to delegate CED authority PASSED.
advisoryNotes: Cross-reference only. Handle any personnel grievance through Employee Relations channel (Steve Niemann, 816-926-6448), not the STC appeal docket.
issueSummary: NOT AN APPEAL — retained here for cross-reference only. March 17 special meeting ceased administrative leave action against 5 Otero/Crowley COC members and delegated daily operations to the CED. April 23 agenda includes a status report on COC operations continuity, not an appeal hearing. If any of the 5 members ultimately file a personal grievance under 7 CFR § 7.28(b), that would be a separate adverse-action appeal handled through Employee Relations (Steve Niemann, FPAC-FBC), not the STC CRP/NAP appeal track.
fullText: Background: Administrative Leave Letters for 5 Otero/Crowley COC members (Knapp, Walter Jr, Hanagan, Tecklenburg, Mason) were prepared by Steve Niemann (HR Specialist, FPAC-FBC) and forwarded to the STC in March 2026. At the March 17 special meeting the STC voted to (1) send a cease-administrative-action letter, and (2) delegate daily operations to the CED pending resolution. The April 23 agenda item is a status report, not an appeal. The appeal-track citations to § 7.28(b) (advance written notice, right to reply, right of further review) apply only if any of the 5 members formally contests the original adverse action — no such filing has been received as of this record.
theGood: STC acted promptly on March 17 to preserve county-office operations via CED delegation
No individual § 7.28(b) grievance has been received — the matter has not escalated to an appeal
theBad: If the cease-action is later reversed by the FSA Administrator under § 7.1(d), the STC should be prepared to re-open the personnel file

redFlags.text: If any of the 5 members files a § 7.28(b) reply or appeal, it must be handled as a confidential personnel matter — NOT processed through the CRP/NAP appeal track.
April 23 agenda should confirm: all 5 letters delivered and tracked, CED notified of delegation scope, Niemann/Sonnenberg/DC reporting complete, Alisha Knapp 0-KB file resolved before delivery.

commonSense: Keep this record cross-referenced but do not conflate it with producer program appeals. Separate decision-making frameworks apply.

resolutionOptions.label: Status Report Only (no vote required)
resolutionOptions.description: Accept staff status report at April 23 meeting. No motion needed unless reinstatement timeline or CED delegation rescission is on the agenda.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: None
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: N/A

resolutionOptions.label: Motion: Set COC Reinstatement Effective Date
resolutionOptions.description: If staff reports the 5 members are ready to resume duties, STC motion to set effective date and clarify CED delegation rollback under 7 CFR § 7.23.
resolutionOptions.fsaRisk: Low — routine
resolutionOptions.nadRisk: N/A
